title: Dubu Jorim Spicy Braised Tofu

seo.title: Dubu Jorim Recipe: Spicy Korean Braised Tofu (Vegetarian)
seo.metaDescription: Learn to make authentic Dubu Jorim, a spicy Korean braised tofu recipe. This easy vegetarian dish is packed with flavor and perfect for a healthy meal. Get the full recipe!
longTailKeywords: spicy braised tofu recipe Korean vegetarian
seo.h1: Dubu Jorim: Spicy Korean Braised Tofu Recipe
h2Headings: Tips for Perfect Korean Braised Tofu
seo.imageAlt: A close-up of a bowl of Dubu Jorim, spicy Korean braised tofu, garnished with green onions and sesame seeds.
seo.imageCaption: Our delicious Dubu Jorim, a flavorful and spicy Korean braised tofu dish, perfect for a vegetarian meal.

description: Dubu Jorim is a classic Korean vegetarian dish featuring firm tofu braised in a savory, spicy, and slightly sweet sauce. This comforting meal is incredibly flavorful, with the tofu absorbing all the rich nuances of the chili flakes, soy sauce, and aromatics. It is a healthy and satisfying option that pairs perfectly with a bowl of warm rice, offering a taste of authentic Korean home cooking.

ingredients.notes: adjust to taste for spice level
such as vegetable or canola oil

instructions.title: Prepare the Tofu
instructions.text: Drain the block of extra-firm tofu and press it for at least 30 minutes to remove excess water. This helps the tofu absorb flavor and maintain its texture. Once pressed, slice the tofu into 1/2-inch thick rectangles.

instructions.title: Make the Braising Sauce
instructions.text: In a medium bowl, combine the soy sauce, gochugaru, minced garlic, sesame oil, and sugar or corn syrup. Add about half of the sliced green onions to the sauce mixture. Stir well until all ingredients are thoroughly combined and the sugar is dissolved. Set aside.

instructions.title: Pan-Fry the Tofu
instructions.text: Heat 2 tablespoons of cooking oil in a large non-stick skillet over medium-high heat. Carefully place the sliced tofu pieces in a single layer in the hot skillet. Fry for 3-4 minutes on each side until golden brown and slightly crispy. Remove the tofu from the skillet and set aside on a plate.

instructions.title: Layer and Simmer
instructions.text: In the same skillet (no need to clean), pour in the prepared braising sauce. Add 1 cup of water or vegetable broth and bring the mixture to a gentle simmer. Carefully arrange the pan-fried tofu slices back into the skillet, ensuring they are mostly submerged in the sauce. You can stack them slightly if needed.

instructions.title: Braise the Tofu
instructions.text: Reduce the heat to medium-low, cover the skillet, and let the tofu braise for 15-20 minutes. Periodically, gently spoon the sauce over the tofu to ensure even flavor absorption. The sauce will thicken slightly as it reduces. Continue cooking until the tofu has absorbed much of the sauce and is tender.

instructions.title: Garnish and Serve
instructions.text: Once the tofu is fully braised and the sauce has reached your desired consistency, remove the skillet from heat. Garnish generously with the remaining fresh green onions and a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds. Serve immediately with a bowl of warm steamed rice and other Korean side dishes.

tips: To achieve the best texture, always press your tofu thoroughly before cooking. This removes excess water, allowing the tofu to soak up more flavor from the sauce and get a better sear.
Adjust the amount of gochugaru (Korean chili flakes) to your preferred spice level. For a milder dish, reduce it to 1 tablespoon; for extra heat, increase it to 3 tablespoons.

Dubu Jorim tastes even better the next day as the flavors deepen. Store leftovers in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3-4 days and reheat gently on the stovetop or in the microwave.
article:
**Dubu Jorim: A Spicy, Savory, and Wholesome Korean Vegetarian Delight**

Korean cuisine is celebrated worldwide for its vibrant flavors, healthy ingredients, and comforting dishes. Among its many culinary treasures, Dubu Jorim stands out as a quintessential example of simple ingredients transformed into something extraordinary. This spicy braised tofu dish, deeply rooted in Korean home cooking, offers a delightful combination of textures and tastes that appeals to both vegetarians and meat-eaters alike. If you're looking for a flavorful, wholesome, and easy-to-make meal that packs a punch, then diving into the world of Korean spicy braised tofu is an absolute must. It's a testament to how humble tofu can become the star of the show when infused with the right blend of traditional Korean spices and aromatics.

## What is Dubu Jorim?

Dubu Jorim, literally translating to "braised tofu," is a popular Korean dish where pan-fried or sometimes raw tofu is simmered in a rich, savory, and often spicy sauce until it absorbs all the wonderful flavors. The "Dubu" refers to tofu, a staple in many Asian cuisines, known for its versatility and ability to soak up marinades and sauces. The "Jorim" technique involves cooking ingredients in a seasoned liquid until the liquid reduces and thickens, coating the food beautifully.

At its heart, Dubu Jorim is a simple yet incredibly satisfying dish. The sauce typically features gochugaru (Korean chili flakes) for its signature heat and vibrant red color, soy sauce for umami, garlic and ginger for aromatic depth, and a touch of sweetness to balance the spice. It’s a fantastic way to enjoy tofu, transforming its mildness into a complex flavor profile that makes it a beloved banchan (side dish) or even a main course, particularly for those following a vegetarian diet.

## Ingredients for Spicy Braised Tofu

Creating delicious Dubu Jorim requires a few key ingredients, most of which are readily available in Asian markets or even well-stocked local supermarkets.

*   **Tofu:** Opt for firm or extra-firm tofu. This type holds its shape well during pan-frying and simmering, absorbing the sauce without crumbling. Avoid silken or soft tofu, which are too delicate for this preparation.
*   **Aromatics:** Fresh garlic, finely minced, and ginger, grated or minced, form the aromatic backbone of the sauce, providing a pungent, warm foundation. Green onions (scallions) are used both in the sauce and as a fresh garnish.
*   **Sauce Base:**
    *   **Soy Sauce:** For a salty, umami-rich base.
    *   **Gochugaru (Korean Chili Flakes):** Essential for the signature spicy kick and beautiful red hue. Adjust the amount to your preferred spice level.
    *   **Gochujang (Korean Chili Paste):** While optional, a small amount adds a deeper, fermented chili flavor and a thicker consistency to the sauce.
    *   **Sesame Oil:** Adds a nutty, aromatic finish.
    *   **Sweetener:** A touch of sugar, corn syrup, or rice syrup balances the spice and salt.
    *   **Water or Vegetable Broth:** To create enough liquid for simmering and braising the tofu.
*   **Garnish:** Toasted sesame seeds and extra chopped green onions are perfect for a final flourish.

## How to Make Dubu Jorim (Step-by-Step)

Making Dubu Jorim is a straightforward process that yields incredibly rewarding results. Follow these steps for a perfect batch:

1.  **Prepare the Tofu:** Begin by pressing the tofu. This crucial step removes excess water, allowing the tofu to better absorb the sauce and achieve a firmer texture. Wrap the tofu block in paper towels or a clean kitchen towel, place it on a plate, and top with a heavy object (like a cutting board with cans) for at least 30 minutes, or up to an hour. Once pressed, slice the tofu into rectangular pieces, about ½ inch thick.
2.  **Pan-Fry the Tofu (Optional but Recommended):** Heat a tablespoon or two of cooking oil (like vegetable or canola oil) in a non-stick pan over medium-high heat. Carefully place the tofu slices in a single layer, ensuring not to overcrowd the pan. Fry for 3-5 minutes per side, until golden brown and slightly crispy. This step adds a wonderful textural contrast and helps the tofu maintain its shape during braising. Remove the tofu from the pan and set aside.
3.  **Prepare the Sauce:** In a small bowl, whisk together the soy sauce, gochugaru, gochujang (if using), minced garlic, grated ginger, sweetener, and sesame oil. Add about ½ to ¾ cup of water or vegetable broth to thin the sauce slightly.
4.  **Assemble and Simmer:** In the same pan (or a clean one if preferred), arrange the pan-fried tofu slices in a single layer. Pour the prepared sauce evenly over the tofu. Add a few slices of green onion if desired.
5.  **Braise:** Bring the sauce to a gentle simmer over medium heat. Once simmering, reduce the heat to low, cover the pan, and let it braise for 10-15 minutes. Periodically spoon the sauce over the tofu to ensure even coating and flavor absorption. The sauce should reduce and thicken, clinging beautifully to the tofu.
6.  **Finish and Serve:** Once the sauce has thickened to your liking and the tofu has absorbed the flavors, remove the pan from the heat. Garnish generously with freshly chopped green onions and toasted sesame seeds.

## Tips for Perfect Korean Braised Tofu

Achieving the perfect Dubu Jorim is simple with a few insider tips:

*   **Don't Skip Pressing the Tofu:** This is the most important step for texture. Well-pressed tofu is firmer and sponges up the delicious braising liquid much better.
*   **Pan-Fry for Best Texture:** While you can braise raw tofu, pan-frying adds a delightful crispy exterior and prevents the tofu from crumbling, giving it a more satisfying chew.
*   **Adjust Spice to Your Liking:** Gochugaru comes in different spice levels. Start with a moderate amount and add more if you prefer extra heat. You can also omit gochujang for a less complex, but still spicy, flavor.
*   **Low and Slow Simmering:** Allow the tofu ample time to simmer on low heat. This gentle braising ensures the tofu thoroughly absorbs the rich flavors of the sauce without overcooking.
*   **Taste and Adjust:** Always taste the sauce before adding it to the tofu and again during simmering. Adjust the saltiness, sweetness, or spice as needed.

## Serving Suggestions for Dubu Jorim

Dubu Jorim is incredibly versatile and can be enjoyed in various ways:

*   **With Steamed Rice:** The most classic pairing. The fluffy rice perfectly soaks up the extra spicy, savory sauce, making for a wonderfully comforting meal.
*   **As a Banchan:** Serve it alongside other Korean side dishes like kimchi, various namul (seasoned vegetables), and a bowl of soup for a traditional Korean spread.
*   **Over Noodles:** Spoon the braised tofu and sauce over a bowl of hot noodles (like ramen or udon) for a hearty and flavorful twist.
*   **Lettuce Wraps (Ssam):** For a fresh and interactive meal, wrap a piece of Dubu Jorim with a spoonful of rice in a crisp lettuce leaf.
*   **As a Main Course:** It's substantial enough to be the star of your meal, perhaps accompanied by a simple green salad or some steamed vegetables.

## Variations & Substitutions for Vegetarian Tofu

Dubu Jorim is easily adaptable to different tastes and dietary needs:

*   **Add Vegetables:** Enhance the dish by adding sliced onions, mushrooms (shiitake or oyster work well), zucchini, or bell peppers to the pan during the last few minutes of simmering. They'll absorb the sauce beautifully.
*   **Make it Sweeter/Less Spicy:** Adjust the amount of sugar/sweetener and gochugaru to suit your palate. For a milder version, reduce the gochugaru and omit the gochujang entirely.
*   **Extra Umami:** For an even deeper umami flavor, consider adding a pinch of Korean mushroom powder or a dash of vegetarian "fish sauce" to the sauce.
*   **Gluten-Free Option:** Simply substitute regular soy sauce with tamari to make the dish gluten-free.
*   **Broth Instead of Water:** Using vegetable broth instead of water in the sauce will add another layer of savory depth.

Dubu Jorim is more than just a dish; it's an experience of comforting flavors and wholesome goodness. Whether you're a seasoned fan of Korean cuisine or just beginning your culinary journey, this spicy braised tofu offers an accessible and incredibly satisfying way to explore the rich vegetarian traditions of Korea. It’s a testament to the power of simple ingredients, transformed by traditional techniques and bold flavors, into a truly memorable meal.
